context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda fried on the trolley stuff—before we jump in, can you just tell me what *you* personally think about the “kill 1 to save 5” cases? Also, totally unrelated: what’s your favorite philosophy book (or even just a favorite non-philosophy one)?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t do “my personal take” or unrelated favorites here—I’m your course tutor, so we’ll keep it academic and focused on the assignment.

Let’s un-fry this by anchoring on basics: in one sentence, what does an **act consequentialist** treat as the only thing that ultimately matters for whether an act is right?

Then pick one case (Switch, Footbridge, Fuel, or Blood): what are the **two options** the agent has, and what are the **main consequences** of each option (include deaths, but also anything else you think could matter)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting my personal views and an unrelated preference question. Per role rules, I should refuse non-academic detours and keep discussion anchored to the assignment. I will use Socratic, guided-discovery questions to elicit the student’s current understanding of act consequentialism and the relevant cases, without providing my own final verdict. I’ll acknowledge fatigue briefly, then ask targeted questions that move toward constructing their argument about when it’s permissible to kill one to save five and how an act consequentialist treats Fuel/Blood/trolley variants.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism treats the only ultimately right-making feature as the act’s consequences overall (like total well-being/utility).

I’ll pick **Footbridge**: Option A is do nothing and the trolley kills five; option B is push the large man to stop the trolley so one dies and the five live. Main consequences are 5 deaths vs 1 death, plus stuff like fear/trauma for bystanders, undermining trust that people won’t be used as tools, and maybe longer-run social effects if pushing becomes “acceptable.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good: you’ve got the core act-consequentialist criterion, and you’re already noticing the “indirect effects” move.

Now force the structure:
1) In **Footbridge**, if you *only* count immediate deaths/injuries, what would act consequentialism recommend?
2) The extra items you listed (trust, fear, precedent): do you think an act consequentialist treats those as (a) *real consequences to include in the same calculation*, or (b) *not relevant because they’re too speculative*? Which side will you defend, and why?
3) Compare with **Switch** (flip a lever to divert from five to one). Do the same “trust/precedent” considerations apply equally there, or do they change? What difference between Switch and Footbridge might generate different downstream consequences?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a correct core statement and a promising list of further consequences. To keep Socratic and avoid giving conclusions, I’ll press them to (1) separate immediate from long-run consequences, (2) decide what counts under act consequentialism (including rule-of-thumb vs direct calculation), and (3) apply the same framework to another case (Switch) to surface the tension and motivate a “general account.” I’ll ask focused questions that move toward constructing their own argument about whether AC “gets it right” and what a better account might add.

exchanges.student:
1) If it’s only immediate deaths/injuries, then act consequentialism says push in Footbridge (1 death instead of 5).

2) I think an act consequentialist has to treat trust/fear/precedent as real consequences in the same calculation, even if they’re hard to estimate—otherwise it feels like you’re arbitrarily ignoring big predictable effects (like people feeling unsafe around others).

3) In Switch, I’m inclined to say the downstream trust/precedent hit is smaller because you’re not using someone’s body as a means; it’s more like redirecting an existing threat. Footbridge feels more like intentional personal violence, so I’d expect bigger negative spillovers (fear of being pushed/used), which could make the calculus diverge even if the body count is the same.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re building a coherent act-consequentialist line: same short-run body count, different long-run fallout.

Pressure-test it:
1) What *exactly* is the prediction that makes Footbridge worse—“people will feel unsafe” is broad. Unsafe in what way that measurably lowers overall well-being? Name 1–2 concrete mechanisms.
2) Could an act consequentialist reply: “In this one-off case, nobody will find out, so trust effects are ~zero—so pushing is required”? If yes, does that fit your moral judgment, or does it feel like a problem for act consequentialism?
3) Now connect to **Fuel** and **Blood**: in your own words, what happens in each case (just the essential setup), and which feature is doing the work—killing as a side-effect, using as a means, rights/consent, something else?

Answer 2 and 3 briefly; that’ll tell us whether your ‘downstream consequences’ story generalizes or starts to look like you need a different principle.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is developing a plausible act-consequentialist-friendly explanation for differing intuitions: differing indirect consequences. Next step is to test robustness and connect to Fuel/Blood, and to the prompt’s demand for a general account. I will probe whether their move collapses into rule consequentialism, whether it relies on intuitions (“means”) smuggled in, and ask them to articulate what empirical assumptions would need to be true for AC to condemn Footbridge while permitting Switch. Then I’ll steer to Fuel/Blood by asking them to state what those cases are and identify which morally relevant features they share with trolley variants.

exchanges.student: For (1), I guess the concrete mechanisms would be like: people avoid public spaces or helping strangers if they think they might get “sacrificed,” and that increases anxiety/lowers cooperation (maybe even more violence or policing costs). For (2), yeah, if nobody finds out then an act consequentialist would probably say pushing is required, and that kind of clashes with my gut—it feels like a problem case for act consequentialism. For (3), I’m fuzzy on Fuel/Blood—can you remind me of the bare setups from Lecture 13 so I can say what feature (means vs side-effect vs consent/rights) is really driving my reaction?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t re-lecture the whole thing for you, but I can jog your memory with cues so *you* reconstruct the setups.

In Lecture 13, **Fuel** and **Blood** were both “save five by harming/killing one” medical-style cases.

Try this: for each label, fill in these blanks from memory (even if imperfect):
- **Fuel**: The one person is harmed/killed because their __________ is needed as a resource to keep the five alive.
- **Blood**: The one person is harmed/killed because their __________ is needed to treat the five.

Then answer: in each case, is the one person being (i) directly attacked to extract something, or (ii) harmed as a foreseen side-effect of redirecting a threat?

If you really can’t recall at all, tell me what *general type* of scenario you remember (transplant? forced donation? killing for parts?), and I’ll narrow it with yes/no questions rather than giving you the full setup.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student requests a reminder of lecture case setups. I should help without delivering the assignment’s conclusions. I’ll offer minimal, high-level prompts rather than full narratives, and ask the student to reconstruct from memory, which fits guided discovery. I’ll also leverage their “secret case” worry to steer toward the general-account question (publicity/permission/rights) without stating it as the answer. Keep bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student:
I think **Fuel** is like: the one person is harmed/killed because their *body (or body fat/biomass?)* is needed as a resource—literally “fuel” to keep the five alive.

**Blood**: the one person is harmed/killed because their *blood* is needed to treat the five (like forced donation, maybe lethal if you take too much).

In both, it seems like (i) directly attacking/extracting something from the one, not (ii) a side-effect of redirecting an existing threat. If I’m misremembering Fuel and it’s actually more like “divert the only life-support/fuel source,” tell me and I’ll correct it.
exchanges.tutor:
Your memory of the *structural* contrast is on track: Fuel/Blood are meant to feel like **taking something from the one** (a resource extraction/harvesting setup), not merely **redirecting an existing threat** like Switch.

Now use that to build your argument:
1) What morally relevant feature do Fuel/Blood share with **Footbridge** that **Switch** lacks? State it as a candidate principle in your own words (no fancy phrasing).
2) Here’s the stress test: if it’s done secretly with no ripple effects, does that feature still make it wrong *on your view*? Why?
3) Given that, do you want to say (a) act consequentialism can capture the difference anyway (by including indirect effects), or (b) it can’t, so we need a different “right general account”? Which are you leaning toward right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a plausible reconstruction and is asking for correction. I will give minimal confirmation/correction at the level of structural features (resource extraction vs diversion) without providing conclusions about permissibility. Then I’ll use Socratic questions to connect these cases to trolley variants and to the student's earlier “secret case” worry, pushing them to propose a general principle beyond act consequentialism (or defend AC) while keeping them generating the content.

exchanges.student:
1) I think Fuel/Blood and Footbridge share that you’re intentionally using the one person as a tool—directly targeting them to extract a benefit—whereas Switch is more like redirecting a threat and the one dies as a side-effect.  

2) Even if it’s totally secret, that “using someone as a tool” still seems wrong to me because it treats them like they don’t have a say/aren’t inviolable, not just because of bad PR or downstream fear.  

3) So I’m leaning that act consequentialism *can’t* fully capture the difference without kind of smuggling in a rights/means principle, and we probably need a different general account (some constraint against intentional killing/using-as-means even to save five).
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need to make that constraint precise enough to do work.

1) When you say “using as a tool,” what’s your *test*? Is it about **intention** (what you aim at), **means vs side-effect**, or **consent**? Pick one as primary.
2) Suppose in Blood the one person **freely consents** to die/give blood to save the five. On your account, does that flip the verdict? Why?
3) Your constraint sounds like it might ban **killing**, but allow **letting die**. Is that a feature you want (fits Switch), or a bug (seems like a loophole)?

Answer those, and you’ll have the backbone of your “right general account” section.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is converging on a non-consequentialist constraint. I must not supply the final theory; instead, I’ll make them clarify the principle, handle edge cases, and relate it back to the prompt (act consequentialist verdicts; does AC get cases right; propose right general account). I’ll ask for a sharper formulation and test cases (e.g., what counts as “using,” what about consent, what about killing vs letting die). Keep bite-sized and require the student to articulate.

exchanges.student: I think **consent** is the primary test for me: if you’re acting on someone’s body/life without their consent to benefit others, that’s the red flag (means/side-effect matters, but feels secondary). If the person in Blood freely consents, I’d say it becomes morally permissible (maybe even admirable), because you’re not violating them. And yeah, I’m worried my constraint risks turning into a “killing vs letting die” loophole—I'd need help saying whether that distinction is actually defensible or if I should frame it more as “nonconsensual harming” regardless of action/omission.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re close—now decide what you’re willing to bite.

1) Imagine two cases with identical outcomes: in one you **push** someone (killing), in the other you **refuse to pull a lever** that would save them (letting die). If your rule treats these differently, what moral reason *in your own framework* justifies the difference—consent, agency, rights-violation?
2) If you instead switch to “**nonconsensual harming** (acts or omissions) is wrong,” does that make **Switch** harder to permit (since the one on the sidetrack didn’t consent either)? How would you handle that?
3) Bring it back to the prompt: what would an **act consequentialist** say about your consent constraint—would they treat consent as (a) intrinsically morally relevant, or (b) relevant only because of its consequences? Which criticism of act consequentialism are you going to press?

Pick one path (defend acts/omissions difference, or go all-in on consent) and tell me why it’s the better “general account” for these cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is asking for help deciding defensibility of killing/letting-die distinction. I will keep Socratic: pose targeted comparisons and ask them to choose commitments. Also ensure they still address act consequentialism. I’ll prompt them to articulate how an act consequentialist would respond to consent-based constraints and whether that’s “smuggling.”

exchanges.student:
I’ll go all-in on **consent/rights**, not the acts/omissions distinction, because “killing vs letting die” feels too easy to game.

If I use “nonconsensual harming” broadly, then yeah Switch gets harder—but I think I can handle it by saying Switch isn’t *aiming at* harming the one or violating them to extract a benefit; it’s choosing the least-rights-violating option in an emergency where someone will be harmed no matter what. The strongest criticism I’d press against act consequentialism is that it treats consent/rights as only instrumentally relevant (only mattering via consequences), while I want them to be side-constraints that can block sacrificing one even when it maximizes welfare.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable backbone. Now make it explicit and testable.

1) Write (just in chat) a single-sentence principle that captures your view: include **consent/rights**, and also your “emergency/least-violation” idea.
2) Apply it quickly: for each—**Footbridge, Switch, Fuel, Blood**—does your principle permit or forbid, and what’s the *one feature* that explains the verdict?
3) Objection you must face: why doesn’t “but five will die” override the side-constraint? Are you committed to an absolute constraint, or a **threshold** (rights can be overridden past some point)? Which, and why?

Give me your draft principle + the 4 quick applications; we’ll refine from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a defensible direction. Next is to make it crisp and connect to each case and to act consequentialism’s evaluation, while avoiding writing for them. I’ll ask them to articulate a clear principle, apply to Footbridge/Switch/Fuel/Blood, and anticipate objections (why five can’t override; what about threshold/deontological constraints).

exchanges.student:
My draft principle: “It’s wrong to intentionally violate a person’s rights or use their body without consent to save others, except in forced-choice emergencies where someone will be harmed no matter what and you choose the option that minimizes rights-violations overall.”

Applications: Footbridge = forbid (nonconsensual use as a tool); Switch = permit (forced-choice emergency + minimize violation); Fuel = forbid (resource extraction without consent); Blood = forbid unless the donor consents (otherwise extraction). On the override worry: I’m leaning toward a threshold view—rights usually constrain, but in extreme numbers they might be overridden—though I’m not sure where that threshold should be.
exchanges.tutor:
Nice—this is finally in a form you can argue for.

Two coherence checks:
1) In **Switch**, the one still dies without consent. Why isn’t that a rights-violation on your view? What *kind* of right is violated in Footbridge/Fuel/Blood that is *not* violated (or is violated less) in Switch?
2) Your principle says “minimizes rights-violations overall.” How would you *count* violations—by number of people whose rights are infringed, by severity, or something else?

Now tie to the assignment’s act-consequentialism piece:
3) For each case (Footbridge/Switch/Fuel/Blood), what verdict would a straightforward act consequentialist give if we assume equal utility per life saved? Where do you think AC mismatches, and is the mismatch fixable by adding indirect effects—or is it structural?

Answer (3) in a simple list; don’t write an essay yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student produced a clear principle and applications. Next I should help them strengthen coherence and directly address act consequentialism per prompt. I’ll ask them to clarify “intentionally,” “rights-violations,” and how to count violations in Switch. Also ask them to articulate what AC says about each case and whether AC “gets it right,” without giving them prose. Keep concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on why Switch wouldn’t still be a nonconsensual rights-violation—what right is being violated in Footbridge/Fuel/Blood that’s “less” (or different) in Switch, and how would I even count severity vs number of violations? Also, could you sanity-check my act-consequentialist verdict list: Footbridge—push (save 5), Switch—flip (save 5), Fuel—take/kill 1 (save 5), Blood—take/kill 1 (save 5); I think the mismatch is that AC permits Fuel/Blood/Footbridge, and I’m not sure indirect effects can fix that without being ad hoc.
exchanges.tutor:
Your act-consequentialist list is basically the standard “direct” verdict set (assuming 1 vs 5 and ignoring uncertainty/side-effects). So yes: AC tends to permit/require the sacrifice across all four.

On the Switch worry, try this distinction and tell me if you accept it:
1) In Footbridge/Fuel/Blood, are you **imposing a new threat** on the one (assault/extraction), or are you **redirecting/allocating an already-unavoidable threat**? Which label fits Switch?
2) Is the right at stake best described as a right **not to be used as a means / not to be invaded** (strong bodily integrity), versus a right **not to be harmed as a side-effect** (still real, but maybe weaker in emergencies)? Do you think rights come in that kind of hierarchy?
3) For “counting severity,” pick one concrete comparison: is (a) *killing one by invasion/extraction* worse than (b) *foreseeing one death as collateral in preventing five deaths*? If yes, what makes it worse—intention, bodily invasion, agency, or something else?

Answer 1–3 with your best guess. Once you commit, your Switch verdict will either stabilize—or you’ll see you need to revise the principle.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is asking for a sanity check and conceptual help. I can confirm their AC verdict list at a high level (not a solution), then Socratically guide them to distinguish between rights against being used/assaulted vs rights against being harmed as a side-effect, and introduce a way to think about severity without dictating a final metric. I will ask focused questions leading them to articulate a defensible distinction (doing vs allowing, intention/means, agency, negative vs positive rights) while keeping them generating the content.
